name: Aggregat 4 / V-2 Rocket
summary: The V-2 was the world's first long-range guided ballistic missile and the first man-made object to reach space. Developed at Peenemünde under Wernher von Braun, it travelled at supersonic speeds, making it impossible to intercept with any technology of the era. Approximately 20,000 forced labourers died producing the rockets — more than the 9,000 civilians killed by V-2 strikes. Its technology directly influenced both the American and Soviet postwar space programmes.

name: Fieseler Fi 103 / V-1 Flying Bomb
summary: The V-1 was the world's first cruise missile and the first of Hitler's Vergeltungswaffe ('Vengeance Weapons'). Powered by a pulsejet engine, its distinctive buzzing sound earned it the British nickname 'Doodlebug' or 'Buzz Bomb'. Launched primarily against London and Antwerp from June 1944, it killed approximately 22,892 people. Allied air defences — including barrage balloons, anti-aircraft guns, and fast fighters — eventually neutralised much of the threat.

images.caption: V-3 (Hochdruckpumpe) site cutaway (digital reconstruction)
images.description: Illustrated cutaway of the angled multi-chamber barrels and underground bunker complex at Mimoyecques, with a location map inset.

name: Hochdruckpumpe (V-3)
summary: The Hochdruckpumpe (High Pressure Pump), designated V-3, was a supergun concept with 130-metre barrels angled toward London, built into a bunker at Mimoyecques, France. Lateral propellant charges along the barrel were meant to accelerate the shell progressively. RAF bombing raids, including bouncing bomb attacks by 617 Squadron, rendered the installation unusable before it could fire a shot. Two shorter versions were used against Luxembourg and Luxemburg, firing 183 rounds.

name: Messerschmitt Me 163 Komet
summary: The Me 163 Komet was the only rocket-powered aircraft to fly combat missions in history. Its maximum speed of nearly 960 km/h made it the fastest aircraft of the war — so fast that Allied pilots reported being unable to track it. However, its powered endurance of only 8 minutes, extreme handling characteristics, and the explosive instability of its C-Stoff/T-Stoff fuel (which ignited on contact with organic material including pilots) made it as dangerous to its crew as to the enemy.

images.caption: Schwerer Gustav railway gun (digital reconstruction)
images.description: Stylized illustration depicting the railway gun and its launch crew in a quarry emplacement.

name: Schwerer Gustav / Dora
summary: Schwerer Gustav was the largest calibre gun ever used in combat and fired the heaviest shells in artillery history. Each shell weighed 7,100 kg and could penetrate 7 metres of concrete. It was used during the Siege of Sevastopol in 1942, firing 48 rounds into Soviet fortifications. The gun required a crew of 2,500 men and special twin railway tracks — its tactical impact was negligible compared to the resources consumed in its construction and deployment.
